contents: International Journal of Clinical Oncology REVIEW ARTICLEIncidence, risk and prognostic role of anti-epidermal growth factor receptor-induced skin rash in biliary cancer: a meta-analysisFangqiang Wei1,2 * Donghun Shin2 * Xiujun Cai1

Received: 27 October 2017 / Accepted: 20 December 2017 (c) Japan Society of Clinical Oncology 2017AbstractBackground Anti-epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR)-induced skin rash is a common adverse event and is considered a prognostic factor of various cancers. However, the role of rash is rarely known in biliary cancer, possibly owing to the low incidence of this frequently fatal malignancy. We thus performed a meta-analysis to investigate the incidence, risk and prognostic significance of skin rash related to anti-EGFR treatment for biliary cancer.Methods Eligible studies were enrolled after a systematic search of electronic databases. A fixed-effects or random-effects model was utilized according to the heterogeneity.Results Fourteen clinical trials published between 2006 and 2017 comprising 1,106 patients with advanced biliary cancer were included. The overall incidence of all-grade and high-grade (grade >=3) rash was 78.2% [95% confidence interval (CI) 70.4-84.3] and 11.3% (7.6-16.5), respectively. Anti-EGFR treatment correlates with a significantly increased risk of all-grade [risk ratio (RR) 7.37, 95% CI 5.11-10.64, p < 0.0001] and high-grade (RR 6.94, 95% CI 1.89-25.45, p = 0.0035) rash compared with control medication. Higher grades of skin rash correlate with a higher objective response rate (RR 3.50, 95% CI 1.47-8.33, p = 0.0048), and a longer overall [hazard ratio (HR) 0.47, 95% CI 0.31-0.71, p = 0.0003) and progression-free survival (HR 0.51, 95% CI 0.36-0.72, p = 0.0001) compared with lower grades or no rash in patients who received anti-EGFR treatment.Conclusions Anti-EGFR treatment correlates with an increased risk of skin rash in advanced biliary cancer. Stratifying patients by the severity of rash may have major implications for survival benefit regarding anti-EGFR treatment for biliary cancer.Keywords Skin rash * Anti-EGFR * Biliary cancer * Survival * Meta-analysisIntroduction

Biliary cancer, including gallbladder carcinoma, cholan-giocarcinoma and cancer of ampulla of Vater, is the second most common primary liver malignancy after hepatocellular carcinoma [1]. Approximately 52,000 new cases of liver and biliary cancer are diagnosed per year in the United States, of which 15,000 are biliary cancer [2]. Although surgery is the only definitive therapy, few patients are able to undergo surgery because of an invariably late diagnosis at advanced stages. Moreover, frequent recurrence after surgery results in an urgent need for improvement in treatment efficacy, with chemotherapy [3, 4] and target therapy [5-9] being increas-ingly investigated.As one of the popular targets, epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), a tyrosine kinase receptor, is frequently overexpressed in human cancer [10]. The pharmacological Electronic supplementary material The online version of this article (http  /s101 47-017-1231 -x) contains supplementary material, which is available to authorized users. * Xiujun Cai   Fangqiang Wei   Donghun Shin  1 Department of General Surgery, Institute of Minimally Invasive Surgery, Sir Run Run Shaw Hospital, School of Medicine, Zhejiang University, No. 3, Qingchun Road, Hangzhou 310016, Zhejiang, China

2 Department of Developmental Biology, Pittsburgh Liver Research Center, McGowan Institute for Regenerative Medicine, School of Medicine, University of Pittsburgh, Pittsburgh, PA 15260, USAblockade of EGFR disrupts cell proliferation, differentia-tion, migration and survival, through its downstream target including JAK (Janus kinase)/STAT3 (signal transducer and activator of transcription 3), MAPK (mitogen-activated pro-tein kinase) and PI3K (phosphatidylinositol-3-kinase)/AKT (protein kinase B) [11, 12]. Clinical benefit of anti-EGFR treatment has been reported in colorectal cancer [13-15], non-small-cell lung cancer [16] and head and neck squa-mous cell carcinoma [17, 18]. However, for advanced bil-iary cancer, the published randomized trials (one phase III [5] and four phase II trials [6-9]) demonstrated no survival benefit of anti-EGFR treatment over chemotherapy. A pos-sible reason for this failure may be the lack of a predictive biomarker that stratifies patients who may acquire survival gain from this novel target therapy.As is common in novel target therapies, anti-EGFR treat-ment is associated with numerous adverse events. Skin rash is a major problem that has been confirmed in systematic studies of various cancers [19-23] and is a prognostic factor for solid tumors including colorectal cancer [20, 21], non-small-cell lung cancer [19, 21] and head and neck squamous cell carcinoma [21]. In fact severe skin rash (grade >=3) has been noted in trials in advanced biliary cancer, with an inci-dence ranging from 2.2-37.1% [5, 6, 8, 9, 24-32]. However, the role of rash has never been systematically studied in bil-iary cancer. Considering the importance of searching for an effective treatment strategy for biliary cancer, the potential prognostic role of skin rash and the small sample size in tri-als, we conducted a meta-analysis of previously published clinical trials to address the incidence, risk and prognostic significance of rash in this setting.

MethodsData sourceThe study was performed in accordance with the guidelines of PRISMA [33] and the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [34]. We carried out a systematic search of PubMed, Web of Science, Cochrane Library data-bases and abstracts from the American Society of Clinical Oncology annual meetings held between January 2013 and August 2017. Search terms included 'cetuximab', 'erlo-tinib', 'panitumumab', 'biliary', 'cholangiocarcinoma' and 'gallbladder'. The language was restricted to English. An additional search from relevant references was manually performed for further supplementation.Study selectionThis study aimed to evaluate the incidence, risk and prog-nostic significance of adverse events of skin rash while undergoing approved anti-EGFR therapies including cetuxi-mab, erlotinib and panitumumab, which are commonly used clinically to treat advanced biliary cancer. Therefore, we included clinical trials and excluded phase I trials that might have relatively variable treatment doses of anti-EGFR thera-pies. Inclusion criteria were prospective clinical trials (phase II or beyond) comprising at least 10 cases with advanced biliary cancer only; anti-EGFR therapies were applied as the only targeted therapy with or without chemotherapy; and reported events or incidents of toxic effect (i.e., skin rash). Trials reporting the same patient cohort were excluded after inclusion of the more recent trial or the one with sufficient information regarding rash.Clinical outcomesThe primary clinical outcomes were all-grade and high-grade (grade >=3) skin rash. Grades of rash were defined according to version 3 or 4 of the Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events (CTCAE). Secondary outcomes were the objective response rate (ORR), overall survival (OS) and progression-free survival (PFS) in anti-EGFR-treated patients categorized by the severity of rash.

Statistical analysisAll analyses were performed using the Comprehensive Meta-Analysis program (version 2; Biostat, Englewood, NJ, USA). Data for numbers of all-grade and high-grade skin rash were extracted. The incidence of patients with rash was calculated along with a 95% confidence interval (CI). In the meta-analysis with a control group, the risk ratio (RR) of skin rash was determined. In the meta-analysis among anti-EGFR-treated patients categorized by the severity of rash, the RR of ORR was also calculated; the hazard ratio (HR) for OS and PFS was either directly extracted or esti-mated based on the methods described by Tierney et al. [35]. Heterogeneity was evaluated by Cochrane's Q statistic. When the p value was < 0.1 the assumption of homogeneity was deemed invalid [36], and a random-effects model was used after meticulous exploration of causes of heterogene-ity. Otherwise, when the p value was > 0.1, a fixed-effects model was preferred. Two-tailed p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. The entire study selection process (including literature search, selection and enrollment) was performed by one of the authors (FQW).ResultsWe yielded 276 articles after the initial literature search, from which 17 potentially relevant clinical trials [5-9, 24-32, 37-39] were further selected for assessment

(Fig. 1). Three trials were excluded because the number of patients was <10 (n = 1) [37]; inclusion of other targeted therapies (n = 1) [38]; and inclusion of the same patient cohort from published data (n = 1) [39]. Ultimately, 14 tri-als [5-9, 24-32] (five randomized trials, nine single-arm trials) published between 2006 and 2017 were enrolled in the meta-analysis. Of the total 1,106 patients with advanced biliary cancer, 848 (76.7%) had cholangiocarcinoma and 237 (21.4%) had gallbladder carcinoma. A total of 767 patients received anti-EGFR treatment with or without chemother-apy. Detailed study characteristics are listed in Table 1.A total of 714 patients who received anti-EGFR treatment from 13 trials were available for analysis of all-grade skin rash, with an incidence ranging from 55.6-94.3%. The het-erogeneity of the 13 trials included was detected (Q = 56.0, p < 0.001). Potential causes for heterogeneity were explored by subgroup analysis of a different type of anti-EGFR treat-ment. We found that heterogeneity exists in erlotinib- or panitumumab-based trials, although it was not detected in cetuximab-based trials (Fig. 2) in which a relative fixed dose was used with similar chemotherapy regimens. The observed heterogeneity was possibly due to a dose difference and different chemotherapy combinations. Using the random-effects model, we found that all-grade skin rash occurred with an incidence of 78.2% (95% CI 70.4-84.3) in patients who received anti-EGFR treatment.

A total of 705 patients who received anti-EGFR treatment from 13 trials were available for analysis of high-grade skin rash, with an incidence ranging from 2.2-37.1%. The het-erogeneity of the 13 trials included was detected (Q = 35.3, p < 0.001). Heterogeneity existed in panitumumab-based studies, but was not detected in erlotinib- or cetuximab-based studies (Figure S1). The random-effects model showed that the incidence of high-grade rash was 11.3% (95% CI 7.6-16.5) in patients who received anti-EGFR treatment.Five randomized trials were available for the meta-anal-ysis of RR for skin rash in patients with advanced biliary cancer compared with controlled trials. As analyzed by the fixed-effects model, the RR was 7.37 (95% CI 5.11-10.64, p < 0.0001) for anti-EGFR therapies versus control medica-tion for all-grade rash, and 6.94 (1.89-25.45, p = 0.0035) for high-grade rash (Fig. 3). In addition, we performed subgroup analysis to further understand the risk of anti-EGFR-induced skin rash. As demonstrated in Table S1, higher risks of rash were observed in patients treated with cetuximab (RR 17.17, 95% CI 7.82-37.46) and in those of Asian origin (RR 11.05, 95% CI 3.20-38.17). On the whole, anti-EGFR treatment was associated with an increased risk of rash in patients with advanced biliary cancer when compared with those treated without anti-EGFR therapies, and the risk of rash varied among different drugs and populations.To better understand the prognostic effects of skin rash in anti-EGFR-treated patients with biliary cancer, a meta-analysis of RR for the ORR, and of HR for the OS and PFS in patients with higher grades of skin rash compared with those with lower grades or no rash, was performed for three randomized trials. Of note, one trial [32] used grade 0 as the control while and the other two [7, 28] used grade 0 or 1 as the control. However, homogeneity of these trials was still achieved (all p > 0.1). The fixed-effects model showed an RR of 3.50 (95% CI 1.47-8.33, p = 0.0048) for ORR (Fig. 4); the HR was 0.47 (95% CI 0.31-0.71, p = 0.0003) for OS and 0.51 (0.36-0.72, p = 0.0001) for PFS in patients receiving anti-EGFR treatment with higher grades versus lower grades of rash or no rash (Fig. 5). Collectively, higher grades of skin rash may correlate with a higher ORR and a longer OS and PFS in anti-EGFR-treated patients with biliary cancer in comparison with lower grades or no rash.

DiscussionThe reason that anti-EGFR-induced skin rash is rarely stud-ied in biliary cancer might be explained in part by the fact that the occurrence of this frequently fatal malignant disease is relatively low despite its increasing incidence [1, 2], and that fewer patients have been receiving this novel target ther-apy compared to those with other types of cancer [19-23]. Therefore, we performed this meta-analysis from 14 clinical  International Journal of Clinical Oncologytrials to render greater statistical power to the pooled data. Our study demonstrates a high overall incidence of all-grade rash [78.2% (95% CI 70.4-84.3)] with anti-EGFR treatment for advanced biliary cancer and an overall incidence of high-grade rash of 11.3% (95% CI 7.6-16.5), which is comparable with studies that reported all-grade and high-grade incidents ranging from 59-80% and 5-12%, respectively, in cetuxi-mab-, erlotinib- or panitumumab-treated patients for other cancers [14, 18, 40, 41]. An overall risk of all-grade (RR 7.37, 95% CI 5.11-10.64; p < 0.0001) and high-grade (RR 6.94, 95% CI 1.89-25.45; p = 0.0035) rash related to anti-EGFR treatment in patients with advanced biliary cancer was also noted. This finding will provide clinicians and patients with more insight into, and a better understanding of, the occurrence and risk regarding anti-EGFR treatment for biliary cancer. Early detection and appropriate manage-ment of rash are essential to reduce morbidity, improve patient quality of life and avoid dose reductions or discon-tinuation of anti-EGFR treatment due to side-effects, which may disrupt the maintenance of an effective dose strategy for anti-tumor efficacy.The mechanisms underlying the association between anti-EGFR treatment and skin rash might be explained by the direct inhibitory effect on the EGFR receptor. EGFR is expressed in proliferating and undifferentiated keratinocytes in the basal epidermal layers and outer layers of hair follicles. Upon inhibition, activated EGFR is abol-ished in epidermal cells, when an effective dose of drug is believed to reach the skin [42]. This results in growth arrest and premature differentiation of the basal keratinocytes fol-lowed by apoptosis and damage as a result of enhanced skin inflammation [43], with interleukin-1 highly expressed in the epidermis and considered a candidate cytokine related to rash development [44]. This induced rash, also termed folliculitis, acneiform rash or acne-like rash, is actually a dermatologic reaction to EGRF inhibition initially occur-ring as inflammatory papulopustular eruptions on the face and upper trunk as early as 7-10 days after treatment [45], which can extend throughout the body in some patients. The rash peaks at 2-4 weeks and thereafter slowly abates with continuation of anti-EGFR treatment [46].It is noteworthy that risk factors for skin rash related to anti-EGFR treatment remain equivocal. Patients with a better performance status [47, 48], male gender [49], younger age [48, 49], and less exposure to smoking [47, 49] are considered more likely to develop rash or severe rash. However, a younger age was proposed in another study to negatively correlate with severe rash [50]. In the present analysis, we demonstrated that patients with biliary cancer treated with cetuximab had the highest RR of 17.17 (95% CI 7.82-37.46) of developing skin rash. Interestingly, the highest incidence (81.3%, 95% CI 76.0-85.6) of rash is also noted in cetuximab-treated patients. These findings are supported by the fact that risks might vary according to different EGFR inhibitor types [23]. In addition, we showed that the Asian population had a relatively higher RR (11.05, 95% CI 3.20-38.17) than the European popula-tion (RR 6.71, 95% CI 4.07-11.07), which might indicate

a better response to EGFR inhibitors in Asian patients with biliary cancer. However, in another study [23] of multiple cancers (mostly lung cancer), the Asian population had an RR (5.85, 95% CI 3.52-9.73) lower than that in non-Asians (RR 8.72, 6.19-12.31), albeit insignificant (p > 0.05). A possible explanation for this difference could be that various EGFR inhibitors were applied for different types of tumor in these studies.Although the incidence and risk of skin rash are high during anti-EGFR treatment, therapies for rash still remain elusive. Guidelines based on evidence are still lacking, with most currently favouring supportive treatment of topical and systemic medications, including moisturizers, corticoster-oids and antibiotics. Basic principles including taking drugs on an empty stomach, avoiding alcohol-containing agents and avoiding sun exposure apply to all patients [51]. Vitamin K3 (menadione) was considered an effective therapy based on mechanisms to circumvent EGFR inhibition by increas-ing re-phosphorylation of the EGFR [43]. However, a recent phase II trial did not show any benefit of vitamin K3 cream for cetuximab-induced rash, and no immunohistochemical findings of restoration of phosphorylated EGFR were found [52]. A recent systematic study focusing on colorectal cancer and lung cancer showed that patients treated with prophy-lactic antibiotics including tetracycline, minocycline and doxycycline had a lower risk of anti-EGFR-induced rash development (odds ratio 0.53, 95% CI 0.39-0.72, p < 0.1) compared with those without antibiotic prophylaxis [22]. The incidence was reduced by 10 and 26% for all-grade and high-grade rash, respectively, in patients treated with pro-phylactic antibiotics [22]. The effectiveness of these antibi-otics might be attributed to their anti-inflammatory effects [53], although microbial pathogens are not usually detected from rash eruptions. Data regarding the effect of prophylac-tic antibiotics for reducing skin rash are lacking in the biliary cancer setting. Therefore, future attempts are warranted in order to evaluate and develop a better management strategy for preventing and treating skin rash.Intriguingly, a correlation between skin rash and thera-peutic efficacy has been noted. In fact, prognostic factors underlying anti-EGFR treatment for cancer have long been investigated. KRAS mutation status was considered a strong predictive factor for efficacy and survival during anti-EGFR treatment. In a phase II trial for colorectal liver metastases, a higher ORR (70 vs 41%, odds ratio 3.42, p = 0.0080) was achieved in cetuximab-treated patients with KRAS wild-type tumors than in those with KRAS mutated tumors [54]. Another study of colorectal cancer showed a better PFS for anti-EGFR treatment over chemotherapy in patients with KRAS wild-type tumors (HR 0.68, p = 0.02) but not in patients with KRAS mutated tumors (HR 1.07, p = 0.75) [15]. Incidents of KRAS mutation ranged from 6.7-41% [5-7, 25-28, 32] in biliary cancer. The predictive
International Journal of Clinical Oncology

role of KRAS mutation status in biliary cancer patients after anti-EGFR treatment is not definitively known, but several available studies indicated no correlation between KRAS mutation status and efficacy or survival benefit during anti-EGFR treatment [6, 7, 26, 27]. Malka et al. [6] reported 65% (24 out of 37) of KRAS wild-type patients achieved no disease progression after anti-EGFR treatment for bil-iary cancer, which was comparable to the proportion (63%, 5/8) of the the KRAS mutated patients. Borbath et al. [28] demonstrated that KRAS wild-type patients had median PFS (6.7 vs 7.1 months, p = 0.616) and OS (14.8 vs 18.8 months, p = 0.611) comparable to those KRAS mutated subjects dur-ing anti-EGFR treatment for cholangiocarcinomas. Of note, our study showed that higher grades of rash might indicate better ORR (RR 3.50, p = 0.0048), longer OS (HR 0.47, p = 0.0003) and longer PFS (HR 0.51, p = 0.0001) than lower grades or absence of rash during anti-EGFR treat-ment for biliary cancer. These findings are of great interest, considering that the prognosis of biliary cancer is typically dismal and that reliable predictive biomarkers are lacking. Similar significant results were also reported in other can-cers, with reported RR ranging from 1.50-2.30 for ORR and HR ranging from 0.30-0.60 and 0.50-0.74 for OS and PFS, respectively [19-21]. Whether there is any correlation between gene mutation status (such as KRAS) and grade of rash among biliary cancer after anti-EGFR treatment remains unclear due to a lack of original information from the trials investigating the correlation between gene muta-tion status and its implication on rash. More future studies are warranted to evaluate survival by stratifying patients according to the severity of rash in a larger cohort to fur-ther confirm that higher grades of rash correlate with better response and efficacy among biliary cancer patients, and also to explore potential mechanisms such as gene mutation status underlying this correlation.This study has several limitations. First, this is a meta-analysis carried out at study level, meaning that different confounding factors from the patient level are unable to be assessed and incorporated into the analysis. Second, the incidents of all-grade and high-grade skin rash demon-strated significant heterogeneity among different studies. This might be explained in part by the fact that differences occur in treatment type, drug dose, concomitant chemother-apy, study population, and CTCAE versions for recording adverse events. Third, some studies did not report a correla-tion between rash and clinical benefit, which might lead to reduced power of our stratified analysis to reach a definitive conclusion. However, the current results were still signifi-cant and encouraging. Fourth, two studies [8, 31] recorded the side-effects as skin toxicity instead of rash, which might overestimate the incidence of anti-EGFR-induced rash, although rash is a classical and indispensable stage of anti-EGFR-induced toxic effects on the skin [43]. Finally, the

relatively low incidence of biliary cancer and correspond-ing small sample sizes involved in the current analysis might limit our findings, although pooled data were already endowed with much more statistical power than individual trials.ConclusionA high overall incidence of skin rash was noted in patients during anti-EGFR treatment for advanced biliary cancer. Anti-EGFR treatment correlates with an increased risk of rash in advanced biliary cancer. Patients treated with cetuxi-mab and those from an Asian population are more likely to bear a higher risk. Anti-EGFR-induced rash may also indicate a better therapeutic response and survival. Early detection and proper treatment of rash can not only improve a patient's quality of life but also maintain their compliance with therapy. Future studies are encouraged to identify bet-ter treatment strategies and stratify patients by the severity of skin rash during anti-EGFR treatment for biliary cancer.Acknowledgements This study was supported by the China Scholar-ship Council (CSC no. 201606320156 to FQ Wei).
